elements.sent-2411-001: Let $A\in\C^{n\times n}$ and let $\cX\subset \C^n$ be an $A$-invariant subspace with $\dim \cX=d\geq 1$, corresponding to exterior eigenvalues of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Given an initial subspace $\cV\subset \C^n$ with $\dim \cV=r\geq d$, we search for expansions of $\cV$ of the form $\cV+A(\cW_0)$, where $\cW_0\subset \cV$ is such that $\dim \cW_0\leq d$ and such that the expanded subspace is closer to $\cX$ than the initial $\cV$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that there exist (theoretical) optimal choices of such $\cW_0$, in the sense that $\theta_i(\cX,\cV+A(\cW_0))\leq \theta_i(\cV+A(\cW))$ for every $\cW\subset \cV$ with $\dim \cW\leq d$, where $\theta_i(\cX,\cT)$ denotes the $i$-th principal angle between $\cX$ and $\cT$, for $1\leq i\leq d\leq \dim \cT$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We relate these optimal expansions to block Krylov subspaces generated by $A$ and $\cV$.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show that the corresponding iterative sequence of subspaces constructed in this way approximate $\cX$ arbitrarily well, when $A$ is Hermitian and $\cX$ is simple.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further introduce computable versions of this construction and compute several numerical examples that show the performance of the computable algorithms and test our convergence analysis.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: optimal subspace expansion, eigenvector approximation, block Krylov subspace, projection methods, computable subspace expansion.
elements.sent-2411-009: The approximation of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a large complex matrix $A\in\C^{n\times n}$ is a central topic in numerical linear algebra.
elements.sent-2411-010: Given an initial search subspace $\cV_0\subset \C^n$, there are several projection methods that allow to compute approximations of the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A$ based on $\cV_0$.
elements.sent-2411-011: This has motivated the development of different techniques to expand the search subspace $\cV_0\subset \cV_1(A,\cV_0)$ in such a way that the enlarged subspace provides better approximations of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A$ than the initial $\cV_0$.
elements.sent-2411-012: One popular expansion method is the block Krylov subspace method (for a detailed account on these methods see \cite{Saad,Stew01,vdH02 ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this work we consider (optimal) expansions of the search subspace based on augmentation/deflation techniques that extend the recent results in \cite{Jia22 for the approximation eigenvalues and eigenvectors to the case of simultaneous approximation of the exterior $d$ eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A$ as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this case the eigenvalues $\la_1,\ldots,\la_d$ of the restriction of $A$ to $\cX$ are (some of the) eigenvalues of $A$; we further assume that these are exterior eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, if $A$ is Hermitian we can be interested in computing the smallest eigenvalues of $A$ and their corresponding eigenvectors.
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, if $A=(B^*B)^{1/2}$ is the operator modulus of an arbitrary matrix $B\in \C^{m\times n}$ then we can be interested in computing the largest eigenvalues of $A$ and their corresponding eigenvectors, that are the largest singular values and their corresponding right-singular vectors of $B$ (that generate the so-called dominant subspaces of $B$).
elements.sent-2411-017: In these situations, the information contained in $(\cX,\{\la_i\}_{i=1 ^d)$ can be used to approximate the action of $A$ as a linear operator (for example, if $A=(B^*B)^{1/2}$ then we can use $(\cX,\{\la_i\}_{i=1 ^d)$ to construct optimal low rank approximations of $B$).}}
elements.sent-2411-018: When $A$ is a large matrix the direct computation of $(\cX,\{\la_i\}_{i=1 ^d)$ can require too much time, or memory space, or it could simply be impractical.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus, one of the major tasks in numerical linear algebra is to compute efficient and fast approximations of this data.
elements.sent-2411-020: We are interested in optimal choices of the expanded subspaces $\cV+A(\cW_0)$ (in each step).
elements.sent-2411-021: Hence, we further impose that $\cV+A(\cW_0)$ lies the closest to $\cX$ among subspaces of the form $\cV+A(\cW)$, for $\cW\subset \cV$ with $\dim \cW=d$.
elements.sent-2411-022: As a (vector valued) measure of distance between subspaces we consider the so-called principal angles.
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, we search for $\cW_0\subset \cV$ with $\dim \cW_0=d$ and such that \begin{equation}\label{eq intro 1} \Theta(\cX\coma \cV+A(\cW_0))\leq \Theta(\cX\coma \cV+A(\cW))\end{equation} for every subspace $\cW\subset \cV$ with $\dim \cW=d$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Here $\Theta(\cX,\cY)$ denotes the diagonal matrix with diagonal entries given by the principal angles between $\cX$ and $\cY$, denoted $0\leq \theta_1(\cX,\cY)\leq \ldots\leq \theta_k(\cX,\cY)\leq \pi/2$, where $k=\min\{\dim \cX,\dim\cY\}$.
